title: [WIP] Dont shutdown stores for read-only podman actions
body:
For read-only actions in podman like ps, inspect, and info, we can avoid
the perf penalty for closing storage.  The store will just unlocked.

$ time sudo podman.before inspect 4476 > /dev/null
real 0m7.701s

$ time sudo podman inspect 4476 > /dev/null
real 0m0.874s

Signed-off-by: baude <bbaude@redhat.com>

@mheon dont worry, this is a work in progress and we will sit on it until next week.

I'll let @nalind  decide, but I believe that there is little risk with this.  The only thing that was being synced was an update on who took the locks, this is equivalent of if podman got hit with a kill -9, storage would not be reset.  Now if it is a command that is actually using the disk, it would be a different story.  For certain Podman commands where we never mount the storage, we are just taking the lock, and then writing the lock back to disk.  IF you are the only user of storage, when you exit you could unmount /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay, which is mounted whenever the storage is Inited, unless it is already mounted.

The lock we update in Shutdown() is being updated mainly to update its timestamp, something that other processes would check for, and if the modification time had changed since they last checked, take as an indication that someone had shut down the storage driver on that location.

For drivers like device mapper, this would typically mean that the volume group had been deactivated, and that it would need to be reactivated by reinitializing the lower-level driver before a process that had been using that storage area could do anything with it again.  The storage library uses the lock to detect and handle this.

For overlay, the startup/shutdown mainly deals with setting up a bind mount to control mount propagation, and then unmounting it.  That last bit is especially necessary during integration testing, since leaving it mounted prevents us from cleanly removing the storage directory or volume.

Information about layers, images, and containers is managed separately from the lock that Shutdown() uses, so unless you need to wipe the area clean, you don't need to call Shutdown() when exiting.  But I would expect us to need to adjust our test suites to arrange to have Shutdown() called as part of their post-test cleanup, since they need to wipe the storage area clean.
